questions.question: According to the three-phase model of trauma therapy, what phase does this course focus on?

options.text: Phase 2: Trauma Processing — working through traumatic memories
Phase 3: Integration and Reconnection — rebuilding relationships and meaning
Phase 1: Stabilization and Safety — establishing safety, reducing symptoms, and learning coping strategies
All three phases equally

questions.explanation: This course focuses on Phase 1: Stabilization and Safety. This phase involves establishing safety in daily life, reducing overwhelming symptoms, learning coping strategies, and building a foundation for deeper work. The lesson emphasizes that stabilization IS the work — not preparation for the 'real' work — and research shows it produces measurable improvements in PTSD symptoms on its own.

questions.question: What are the four foundations of stabilization described in the lesson?

options.text: Medication, therapy, journaling, and exercise
Safety, sleep, routine, and basic needs
Avoidance, isolation, medication, and rest
Grounding, breathing, meditation, and yoga

questions.explanation: The four foundations of stabilization are safety (physical and emotional), sleep (consistent schedule, calming routines, safe bedroom environment), routine (predictable daily structure), and basic needs (nutrition, hydration, movement, hygiene). These create the conditions where healing can happen.

questions.question: Why does the lesson say routine and predictability help with trauma recovery?

options.text: Routines distract you from thinking about trauma
Routines build discipline, which is what trauma survivors lack
Predictability helps your nervous system feel safer because there are fewer surprises for your alarm system to react to
Routines are required by therapists before beginning treatment

questions.explanation: Predictability helps your nervous system feel safer. When your days have structure, there are fewer surprises for your alarm system (amygdala) to react to. This reduces the chronic hypervigilance that drains energy and keeps the nervous system in a state of alert.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of numbing disguised as self-care?

options.text: Taking a warm bath after a difficult day
Going for a walk in nature
Using alcohol or excessive screen time to avoid feeling emotions
Journaling about your feelings without judgment

questions.explanation: The lesson distinguishes genuine self-care from numbing. Using substances, excessive screen time, or overwork to avoid feeling emotions is numbing, not self-care. True self-care is gentle, flexible, honest, and sometimes as basic as brushing your teeth and drinking water. It acknowledges and allows feelings rather than suppressing them.

questions.question: What does the lesson say about having a bad day during recovery?

options.text: Bad days mean your coping strategies aren't working and you need to change them
Bad days are a sign you should stop the course and only continue after professional treatment
Bad days don't erase good days — healing is not linear, and a difficult day doesn't mean you've gone backwards
Bad days can be prevented entirely with proper planning and self-care

questions.explanation: Healing is not linear. You may have a great week followed by a terrible day. That terrible day doesn't mean you've gone backwards — it means your nervous system is still learning to regulate. The lesson advises lowering expectations on hard days, doing one small thing, using grounding, reaching out, and reminding yourself 'this will pass.'
